title: Willow window box liner
subtitle: Woven willow surround for a standard window box planter
excerpt: A woven willow liner for a standard window box: four staked sides in pairing weave, no base. The willow stakes are inserted into the soil at the box edges and the weavers wrap around the planter exterior.
sourceNotes: Edlin, H.L., Woodland Crafts in Britain (1949) on willow craft and outdoor basketwork. Caulfield and Saward, Dictionary of Needlework (1882) on woven panels.

glossaryTerms.definition: The willow-basket weave structure where vertical stakes form the skeleton and horizontal weavers pass between them.
A two-weaver technique: two weavers alternate, each crossing in front of one stake and behind the next.
The first few rows of weaving above the base that lock the stakes upright and set the angle of the sides.
Boiled and bark-stripped willow; the standard UK basketry material. Weathers to silver-grey outdoors.
A simple one-rod border: each stake passes in front of one upright and behind the next, then tucks inside.

content.text: A willow window-box liner wraps the outside of an existing planter in woven
buff willow
. There is no base: the stakes are inserted into the soil at the planter edges and the
stake-and-strand
weave builds up around the exterior. A
trac border
finishes the top. This is a beginner woven panel project: the only
pairing
technique is worked on a rectangular form with fixed corners.
Secateurs are sharp. Close them when setting down; keep fingers clear of the cutting path.
Dry willow ends are stiff and springy when trimmed. Guard your face when snipping tall stakes.
Soaking water: do not leave willow soaking for more than 7 days. Stagnant water grows mould on the rods.
Workspace: low stable seating, knees together as a lap-board for the work-in-progress.
Materials

Measure the window box: standard UK size is 60 cm long, 20 cm wide, 20 cm tall. For a liner of this size: 24 stakes at 3 foot (90 cm) length, 3 to 3.5 mm diameter; two bundles of pairers at 4 foot (120 cm) length, 2 to 2.5 mm diameter. Soak all rods for 1 hour before use.
Tools
A bodkin; secateurs; a rapping iron; a soaking trough.
Method
Insert the stakes into the soil around the planter perimeter: one stake per 5 cm, pushed 10 cm into the soil at the planter wall edge. The stakes stand upright against the outside of the planter.
Work the
upset
: two circuits of
pairing
close to the soil surface. Use two weavers simultaneously, alternating them around each stake. The upset locks the stakes upright and sets the angle for the main weave.
Continue pairing up the sides in circuits, adding new weavers as old ones run out. Pack each row down with the rapping iron after two circuits.
At the corners, the stakes are closer together. Work the corner stake last in each circuit; the weaver passes in front of it and behind the first stake on the next face.
Continue weaving to within 5 cm of the stake tips. At this point the stakes have 5 cm of length remaining above the last row of weaving.
Work the trac border: bend the first stake in front of the next stake and behind the one after, then tuck the tip inside the last row. Repeat for each stake in sequence around all four sides.
Finishing

Trim any protruding weaver ends flush with secateurs. The buff willow will weather to silver-grey outdoors over the first summer. No treatment is needed or recommended; preservatives prevent the natural weathering colour.
Variations
Randing weave instead of pairing: use a single weaver for a simpler pattern. Requires an odd number of stakes.
Dark willow for contrast: mix buff willow stakes with brown willow weavers for a two-tone panel.
Wider planter: add additional stakes at the same 5 cm spacing. The pairing technique is identical regardless of planter length.

items.symptom: Stakes lean outward as weaving progresses
items.cause: Upset rows not packed tightly enough at the base
items.fix: Re-work the upset rows, rapping each row very firmly with the rapping iron. Two tight upset rows prevent all subsequent leaning.

items.symptom: Corner gaps widen as weaving progresses
items.cause: Corner stake not treated consistently in each circuit
items.fix: On each circuit, always pass the weaver in front of the corner stake, then immediately behind the first stake on the new face. Consistent treatment at each corner keeps the gap closed.

items.symptom: Trac border snaps when bending down the stakes
items.cause: Stakes dried out before the border was worked
items.fix: Dampen the top 10 cm of each stake with a wet cloth before beginning the trac border.
